[Broxtowe](/wiki/Borough_of_Broxtowe "Borough of Broxtowe") 8. [Rushcliffe](/wiki/Borough_of_Rushcliffe "Borough of Rushcliffe")

([/ˈnɒtɪŋəmʃər, -ʃɪər/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English");[[5]](#cite_note-CollinsDictionary-5) abbreviated *Notts.*) is a [ceremonial county](/wiki/Ceremonial_county "Ceremonial county") in the [East Midlands](/wiki/East_Midlands "East Midlands") of England. The county is bordered by [South Yorkshire](/wiki/South_Yorkshire "South Yorkshire") to the north-west, [Lincolnshire](/wiki/Lincolnshire "Lincolnshire") to the east, [Leicestershire](/wiki/Leicestershire "Leicestershire") to the south, and [Derbyshire](/wiki/Derbyshire "Derbyshire") to the west. The largest settlement is the city of [Nottingham](/wiki/Nottingham "Nottingham").

**Nottinghamshire** ([/ˈnɒtɪŋəmʃər, -ʃɪər/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English");[[5]](#cite_note-CollinsDictionary-5) abbreviated *Notts.*) is a [ceremonial county](/wiki/Ceremonial_county "Ceremonial county") in the [East Midlands](/wiki/East_Midlands "East Midlands") of England. The county is bordered by [South Yorkshire](/wiki/South_Yorkshire "South Yorkshire") to the north-west, [Lincolnshire](/wiki/Lincolnshire "Lincolnshire") to the east, [Leicestershire](/wiki/Leicestershire "Leicestershire") to the south, and [Derbyshire](/wiki/Derbyshire "Derbyshire") to the west. The largest settlement is the city of [Nottingham](/wiki/Nottingham "Nottingham").

The county has an area of 2,160 km2 (830 sq mi) and had an estimated population of 1,188,090 in 2024. Nottingham is in the south-west of the county, which is the most densely populated area. Other settlements include [Worksop](/wiki/Worksop "Worksop") in the north-east, [Newark-on-Trent](/wiki/Newark-on-Trent "Newark-on-Trent") in the east, and [Mansfield](/wiki/Mansfield "Mansfield") in the west. For [local government](/wiki/Local_government_in_England "Local government in England") purposes Nottinghamshire comprises a [non-metropolitan county](/wiki/Non-metropolitan_county "Non-metropolitan county"), with seven districts, and the Nottingham [unitary authority](/wiki/Unitary_authorities_of_England "Unitary authorities of England") area. The [East Midlands Combined County Authority](/wiki/East_Midlands_Combined_County_Authority "East Midlands Combined County Authority") includes [Nottinghamshire County Council](/wiki/Nottinghamshire_County_Council "Nottinghamshire County Council") and [Nottingham City Council](/wiki/Nottingham_City_Council "Nottingham City Council").

The geography of Nottinghamshire is largely defined by the [River Trent](/wiki/River_Trent "River Trent"), which forms a wide valley which crosses the county from the south-west to the north-east. North of this, in the centre of the county, is [Sherwood Forest](/wiki/Sherwood_Forest "Sherwood Forest"), the remnant of a large [ancient woodland](/wiki/Ancient_woodland "Ancient woodland").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nottinghamshire&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Nottinghamshire](/wiki/History_of_Nottinghamshire "History of Nottinghamshire")

Nottinghamshire lies on the [Roman](/wiki/Roman_Britain "Roman Britain") [Fosse Way](/wiki/Fosse_Way "Fosse Way"), and there are Roman settlements in the county; for example at [Mansfield](/wiki/Mansfield "Mansfield"), and forts such as at the [Broxtowe Estate](/wiki/Broxtowe_Estate "Broxtowe Estate") in [Bilborough](/wiki/Bilborough "Bilborough"). The county was settled by [Angles](/wiki/Angles_(tribe) "Angles (tribe)") around the 5th century, and became part of the Kingdom, and later Earldom, of [Mercia](/wiki/Mercia "Mercia"). However, there is evidence of [Saxon](/wiki/Anglo-Saxons "Anglo-Saxons") settlement at the Broxtowe Estate, [Oxton](/wiki/Oxton,_Nottinghamshire "Oxton, Nottinghamshire"), near Nottingham, and [Tuxford](/wiki/Tuxford "Tuxford"), east of [Sherwood Forest](/wiki/Sherwood_Forest "Sherwood Forest"). The name first occurs in 1016, but until 1568, the county was administratively united with Derbyshire, under a single [Sheriff](/wiki/Sheriff "Sheriff"). In [Norman](/wiki/Normans "Normans") times, the county developed [malting](/wiki/Malting "Malting") and [woollen](/wiki/Woollen "Woollen") industries.

The village of [Edwinstowe](/wiki/Edwinstowe "Edwinstowe") close to [Sherwood Forest](/wiki/Sherwood_Forest "Sherwood Forest") took the name from [Edwin of Northumbria](/wiki/Edwin_of_Northumbria "Edwin of Northumbria"), who died in 633 nearby and was provisionally buried in St Mary's Church, [Edwinstowe](/wiki/Edwinstowe "Edwinstowe").[[6]](#cite_note-6)

[William the Conqueror](/wiki/William_the_Conqueror "William the Conqueror") in 1066 made [Sherwood Forest](/wiki/Sherwood_Forest "Sherwood Forest") a [Royal Forest](/wiki/Royal_Forest "Royal Forest") for hunting which was frequently visited by the Mercian and later Kings.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-groves-9)

William's 1086 [Domesday Book](/wiki/Domesday_Book "Domesday Book") identified certain areas in Nottinghamshire being under the land of King [Edward the Confessor](/wiki/Edward_the_Confessor "Edward the Confessor") these included [Mansfield](/wiki/Mansfield "Mansfield") and [Sutton in Ashfield](/wiki/Sutton_in_Ashfield "Sutton in Ashfield"), amongst other places including [Skegby](/wiki/Skegby "Skegby"), [Dunham-on-Trent](/wiki/Dunham-on-Trent "Dunham-on-Trent"), [East Drayton](/wiki/East_Drayton "East Drayton"), [East Markham](/wiki/East_Markham "East Markham"), [Farnsfield](/wiki/Farnsfield "Farnsfield"), [Warsop](/wiki/Warsop "Warsop"), [Carburton](/wiki/Carburton "Carburton"), [Edwinstowe](/wiki/Edwinstowe "Edwinstowe"), [Carlton-on-Trent](/wiki/Carlton-on-Trent "Carlton-on-Trent"), [Budby](/wiki/Budby "Budby"), [Perlethorpe](/wiki/Perlethorpe "Perlethorpe") and [Walesby](/wiki/Walesby,_Nottinghamshire "Walesby, Nottinghamshire").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

[King John's Palace](/wiki/King_John%27s_Palace "King John's Palace") ruin near [Kings Clipstone](/wiki/Kings_Clipstone "Kings Clipstone") was a royal residence for [King John](/wiki/John,_King_of_England "John, King of England"), the area being near to [Sherwood Forest](/wiki/Sherwood_Forest "Sherwood Forest"). [King John's Palace](/wiki/King_John%27s_Palace "King John's Palace") was a place where [William I](/wiki/William_the_Lion "William the Lion") met [Richard I](/wiki/Richard_I_of_England "Richard I of England") to congratulate him on his return from the crusades.[[9]](#cite_note-groves-9) [King John](/wiki/John,_King_of_England "John, King of England") died at [Newark Castle](/wiki/Newark_Castle,_Nottinghamshire "Newark Castle, Nottinghamshire") in 1216.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

During the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), the county held much needed minerals such as [coal](/wiki/Coal "Coal") and [iron ore](/wiki/Iron_ore "Iron ore"), and had constructed some of the first experimental waggonways in the world; an example of this is the [Wollaton](/wiki/Wollaton "Wollaton") wagonway of 1603–1616, which transported minerals from [bell pit](/wiki/Bell_pit "Bell pit") mining areas at [Strelley](/wiki/Strelley "Strelley") and [Bilborough](/wiki/Bilborough "Bilborough"), this led to [canals](/wiki/Canal "Canal") and [railways](/wiki/Railway "Railway") being constructed in the county, and the [lace](/wiki/Lace "Lace") and cotton industries grew. In the 18th and 19th centuries, mechanised deeper [collieries](/wiki/Collieries "Collieries") opened, and mining became an important economic sector. Although there are no remaining working mines in Nottinghamshire now.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Lincolnia_nottinghamia_Atlas.jpg/250px-Lincolnia_nottinghamia_Atlas.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lincolnia_nottinghamia_Atlas.jpg)

Hand-drawn map of Lincolnshire and Nottinghamshire from 1576.

Until 1610, Nottinghamshire was divided into eight [Wapentakes](/wiki/Wapentake "Wapentake"). Sometime between 1610 and 1719, they were reduced to six – [Newark](/wiki/Newark_(wapentake) "Newark (wapentake)"), [Bassetlaw](/wiki/Bassetlaw_(wapentake) "Bassetlaw (wapentake)"), [Thurgarton](/wiki/Thurgarton_(wapentake) "Thurgarton (wapentake)"), [Rushcliffe](/wiki/Rushcliffe_(wapentake) "Rushcliffe (wapentake)"), [Broxtowe](/wiki/Broxtowe_(wapentake) "Broxtowe (wapentake)"), and [Bingham](/wiki/Bingham_(wapentake) "Bingham (wapentake)"), some of these names still being used for the modern districts. Oswaldbeck was absorbed in Bassetlaw, of which it forms the North Clay division, and Lythe in Thurgarton.

[Mansfield](/wiki/Mansfield "Mansfield") and surrounding areas in Nottinghamshire in the 17th century became the centre of [nonconformism](/wiki/Nonconformist_(Protestantism) "Nonconformist (Protestantism)") in separation from the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"). In particular [Mansfield](/wiki/Mansfield "Mansfield") became the birthplace of the [Quakers](/wiki/Quaker "Quaker") with [George Fox](/wiki/George_Fox "George Fox") living in the town.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Nottinghamshire is famous for its involvement with the legend of [Robin Hood](/wiki/Robin_Hood "Robin Hood"). This is also the reason for the numbers of tourists who visit places like [Sherwood Forest](/wiki/Sherwood_Forest "Sherwood Forest"), [City of Nottingham](/wiki/Nottingham "Nottingham"), and the surrounding villages in Sherwood Forest. To reinforce the Robin Hood connection, the [University of Nottingham](/wiki/University_of_Nottingham "University of Nottingham") in 2010 has begun the [Nottingham Caves Survey](/wiki/Nottingham_Caves_Survey "Nottingham Caves Survey"), with the goal "to increase the tourist potential of these sites". The project "will use a 3D laser scanner to produce a three dimensional record of more than 450 sandstone caves around Nottingham".[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Nottinghamshire was mapped first by [Christopher Saxton](/wiki/Christopher_Saxton "Christopher Saxton") in 1576; the first fully surveyed map of the county was by John Chapman, who produced Chapman's Map of Nottinghamshire in 1774.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The map was the earliest printed map at a sufficiently useful scale (one statute mile to one inch) to provide basic information on village layout, and the existence of landscape features such as roads, [milestones](/wiki/Milestone "Milestone"), [tollbars](/wiki/Tollbar "Tollbar"), parkland, and mills.

Nottinghamshire saw a slight change to its overall boundary in the extreme northern part of the county in 1974, when the villages of [Blaxton](/wiki/Blaxton "Blaxton"), [Finningley](/wiki/Finningley "Finningley") and [Auckley](/wiki/Auckley "Auckley") (part) were merged into the Doncaster in [South Yorkshire](/wiki/South_Yorkshire "South Yorkshire").[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## Physical geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nottinghamshire&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Physical geography")]

[![Map](https://maps.wikimedia.org/img/osm-intl,9,53.12,-1.004,300x450.png?lang=en&domain=en.wikipedia.org&title=Nottinghamshire&revid=1344764604&groups=_9f75d1af52e4ea9b5effd81373af80fb2b6c9733&parser=legacy)](/wiki/Special:Map/9/53.12/-1.004/en)

Interactive map of Nottinghamshire and city/districts

Nottinghamshire, like [Derbyshire](/wiki/Derbyshire "Derbyshire"), and South Yorkshire, sits on extensive coal measures, up to 900 [metres](/wiki/Metre "Metre") (3,000 [feet](/wiki/Foot_(unit) "Foot (unit)")) thick, and occurring largely in the north of the county. There is an [oilfield](/wiki/Oilfield "Oilfield") near [Eakring](/wiki/Eakring "Eakring"). These are overlaid by [sandstones](/wiki/Sandstone "Sandstone") and [limestones](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone") in the west, and [clay](/wiki/Clay "Clay") in the east.[[17]](#cite_note-17) The north of the county is part of the [Humberhead Levels](/wiki/Humberhead_Levels "Humberhead Levels") [lacustrine plain](/wiki/Lacustrine_plain "Lacustrine plain"). The centre and south west of the county, around Sherwood Forest, features undulating hills with ancient [oak](/wiki/Oak "Oak") woodland. Principal rivers are the [Trent](/wiki/River_Trent "River Trent"), [Idle](/wiki/River_Idle "River Idle"), [Erewash](/wiki/River_Erewash "River Erewash"), and [Soar](/wiki/River_Soar "River Soar"). The Trent, fed by the Soar, Erewash, and Idle, composed of many streams from Sherwood Forest, run through wide and flat valleys, merging at [Misterton](/wiki/Misterton,_Nottinghamshire "Misterton, Nottinghamshire"). A point just north of Newtonwood Lane, on the boundary with Derbyshire is the highest point in Nottinghamshire; at 205 metres (673 feet),[[18]](#cite_note-18) while [Silverhill](/wiki/Silverhill,_Nottinghamshire "Silverhill, Nottinghamshire"), a [spoil heap](/wiki/Spoil_heap "Spoil heap") left by the former Silverhill colliery, a human-made point often cited as the highest, reaches 204 metres (669 feet). The lowest is Peat Carr, east of Blaxton, at [sea level](/wiki/Sea_level "Sea level"); the Trent is tidal below [Cromwell Lock](/wiki/Cromwell_Lock "Cromwell Lock").[[19]](#cite_note-Experiencing_the_Highs_and_Lows-19)

Nottinghamshire is sheltered by the [Pennines](/wiki/Pennines "Pennines") to the west, so receives relatively low rainfall at 641 to 740 [millimetres](/wiki/Millimetre "Millimetre") (25 to 29 [inches](/wiki/Inch "Inch")) annually.[[20]](#cite_note-20) The average temperature of the county is 8.8–10.1 degrees [Celsius](/wiki/Celsius "Celsius") (48–50 degrees [Fahrenheit](/wiki/Fahrenheit "Fahrenheit")).[[21]](#cite_note-21) The county receives between 1321 and 1470 hours of sunshine per year.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### Green belt

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nottinghamshire&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Green belt")]

Main article: [Nottingham and Derby Green Belt](/wiki/Nottingham_and_Derby_Green_Belt "Nottingham and Derby Green Belt")

Nottinghamshire contains one green belt area, first drawn up from the 1950s. Completely encircling the Nottingham conurbation, it stretches for several miles into the surrounding districts, and extends into Derbyshire.

## Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nottinghamshire&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Politics")]

See also: [Nottinghamshire local elections](/wiki/Nottinghamshire_local_elections "Nottinghamshire local elections")

Nottinghamshire, including the city of Nottingham, is represented by eleven [members of parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom"); nine for the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)"), and two [Reform UK](/wiki/Reform_UK "Reform UK") MPs

Following the [2025 County Council election](/wiki/2025_Nottinghamshire_County_Council_election "2025 Nottinghamshire County Council election"), [Nottinghamshire County Council](/wiki/Nottinghamshire_County_Council "Nottinghamshire County Council") is controlled by Reform UK. The party went from having a single councillor before the election to winning a majority of the council's seats.[[23]](#cite_note-23) The council is currently made up of 41 Reform UK councillors, 18 Conservatives, four Labour and three from local parties.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Prior to the 2025 election, the council had been fully or partially controlled by the Conservative Party since 2017. The council had historically been under Labour control; the party held a majority on the council from 1981 to 2009.

Local government is devolved to seven local borough and district councils. [Ashfield](/wiki/Ashfield_District "Ashfield District") is controlled by the [Ashfield Independents](/wiki/Ashfield_Independents "Ashfield Independents"). [Bassetlaw](/wiki/Bassetlaw_District "Bassetlaw District"), [Gedling](/wiki/Borough_of_Gedling "Borough of Gedling") and [Mansfield](/wiki/Mansfield_District "Mansfield District") are Labour-controlled. [Newark and Sherwood](/wiki/Newark_and_Sherwood "Newark and Sherwood") is controlled by a coalition of Labour, [Liberal Democrat](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)") and independent councillors. [Rushcliffe](/wiki/Borough_of_Rushcliffe "Borough of Rushcliffe") is Conservative-controlled. [Broxtowe](/wiki/Borough_of_Broxtowe "Borough of Broxtowe") is run by a minority [Broxtowe Alliance](/wiki/Broxtowe_Alliance "Broxtowe Alliance") administration. [Nottingham City Council](/wiki/Nottingham_City_Council "Nottingham City Council"), which governs the Nottingham unitary authority and is independent of Nottinghamshire County Council, is majority Labour-controlled.

## Economy and industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nottinghamshire&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Economy and industry")]

The regional economy was traditionally based on industries such as coal mining in the [Leen Valley](/wiki/Leen_Valley "Leen Valley"), and manufacturing. Since the invention of the [knitting frame](/wiki/Knitting_frame "Knitting frame") by local [William Lee](/wiki/William_Lee_(inventor) "William Lee (inventor)"), the county, in particular [Nottingham](/wiki/Nottingham "Nottingham"), became synonymous with the lace industry.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

In 1998, Nottinghamshire had a [gross domestic product](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") (GDP) per-capita of [£](/wiki/Pound_Sterling "Pound Sterling")12,000, and a total GDP of £12,023 million. This is compared to a per-capita GDP of £11,848 for the [East Midlands](/wiki/East_Midlands "East Midlands"), £12,845 for England, and £12,548 for the United Kingdom. Nottingham had a GDP per-capita of £17,373, North Nottinghamshire £10,176, and South Nottinghamshire £8,448.[[27]](#cite_note-27) In October 2005, the United Kingdom had 4.7% unemployment, the East Midlands 4.4%, and the Nottingham commuter belt area 2.4%.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nottinghamshire&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Education")]

See also: [List of schools in Nottinghamshire](/wiki/List_of_schools_in_Nottinghamshire "List of schools in Nottinghamshire")

### Secondary education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nottinghamshire&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Secondary education")]

The county has [comprehensive](/wiki/Comprehensive_school "Comprehensive school") secondary education with 47 state [secondary schools](/wiki/Secondary_school#England_and_Wales "Secondary school"), as well as 10 [private schools](/wiki/Private_schools_in_the_United_Kingdom "Private schools in the United Kingdom"). The City of Nottingham [local education authority](/wiki/Local_education_authority "Local education authority") (LEA) has 18 state schools and six independent schools, not including [sixth form colleges](/wiki/Sixth_form_college "Sixth form college").

A total of 9,700 pupils took [GCSEs](/wiki/GCSE "GCSE") in the Nottinghamshire LEA in 2007. The best results were from the [West Bridgford School](/wiki/West_Bridgford_School "West Bridgford School"), closely followed by [Rushcliffe Spencer Academy](/wiki/Rushcliffe_School "Rushcliffe School") and the Minster School in [Southwell](/wiki/Southwell,_Nottinghamshire "Southwell, Nottinghamshire"). In Nottingham, the best results came from the [Trinity Catholic School](/wiki/Trinity_School_(Nottingham) "Trinity School (Nottingham)") and the Fernwood School in [Wollaton](/wiki/Wollaton "Wollaton").[[29]](#cite_note-29)

At [A-level](/wiki/A-level "A-level"), the highest performing institution was [The Becket School](/wiki/The_Becket_School "The Becket School"), followed by the West Bridgford School. Some of Nottingham city best results tend to come from [Nottingham High School](/wiki/Nottingham_High_School "Nottingham High School"), closely followed by the all-female [Nottingham High School for Girls](/wiki/Nottingham_High_School_for_Girls "Nottingham High School for Girls"), both of which are privately run.

[Worksop College](/wiki/Worksop_College "Worksop College") is another private school near to [Worksop](/wiki/Worksop "Worksop").

### Higher education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nottinghamshire&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Higher education")]

The [University of Nottingham](/wiki/University_of_Nottingham "University of Nottingham") is a [Russell Group](/wiki/Russell_Group "Russell Group") university and well-renowned, offering one of the broadest selections of courses in the UK. [Nottingham Trent University](/wiki/Nottingham_Trent_University "Nottingham Trent University") is one of the most successful [post-1992](/wiki/Further_and_Higher_Education_Act_1992 "Further and Higher Education Act 1992") [universities](/wiki/New_Universities "New Universities") in the UK. Nottingham is home to a campus of the [University of Law](/wiki/University_of_Law "University of Law"). All three of these institutions combine to make Nottingham one of [England](/wiki/England "England")'s largest student cities. Nottingham Trent University also has an agricultural college near [Southwell](/wiki/Southwell,_Nottinghamshire "Southwell, Nottinghamshire"), while the University of Nottingham has one at [Sutton Bonington](/wiki/Sutton_Bonington "Sutton Bonington").

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nottinghamshire&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Larwood.jpg/250px-Larwood.jpg)](/wiki/File:Larwood.jpg)

National and County cricket player [Harold Larwood](/wiki/Harold_Larwood "Harold Larwood")

Nottinghamshire is home to the [Sherwood Forest](/wiki/Sherwood_Forest "Sherwood Forest"), known for its association with the legend of [Robin Hood](/wiki/Robin_Hood "Robin Hood").[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)

Nottinghamshire contains the ancestral home of the poet [Lord Byron](/wiki/Lord_Byron "Lord Byron"), [Newstead Abbey](/wiki/Newstead_Abbey "Newstead Abbey"), which he sold in 1818. It is now owned by Nottingham City Council, and is open to the public. The acclaimed author [D. H. Lawrence](/wiki/D._H._Lawrence "D. H. Lawrence") was from [Eastwood](/wiki/Eastwood,_Nottinghamshire "Eastwood, Nottinghamshire") in Nottinghamshire. [Toton](/wiki/Toton "Toton") was the birthplace and home of English folk singer-songwriter [Anne Briggs](/wiki/Anne_Briggs "Anne Briggs"), well known for her song *Black Waterside*. The north of the county is also noteworthy for its connections with the [Pilgrim Fathers](/wiki/Pilgrim_(Plymouth_Colony) "Pilgrim (Plymouth Colony)"). [William Brewster](/wiki/William_Brewster_(Pilgrim) "William Brewster (Pilgrim)"), for example, came from the village of Scrooby, and was influenced by [Richard Clyfton](/wiki/Richard_Clyfton "Richard Clyfton"), who preached at [Babworth](/wiki/Babworth "Babworth").

Nottinghamshire has international [twinning](/wiki/Town_twinning "Town twinning") arrangements with the province of [Wielkopolska](/wiki/Greater_Poland_Voivodeship "Greater Poland Voivodeship") ([Greater Poland](/wiki/Greater_Poland "Greater Poland")) in western [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland"), and with the province's capital city, [Poznań](/wiki/Pozna%C5%84 "Poznań").[[32]](#cite_note-32)

In 2002, *[Crocus nudiflorus](/wiki/Crocus_nudiflorus "Crocus nudiflorus")* (Autumn crocus) was voted by the public as the county flower of Nottinghamshire.[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34)

### Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nottinghamshire&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Sport")]

[Nottinghamshire County Cricket Club](/wiki/Nottinghamshire_County_Cricket_Club "Nottinghamshire County Cricket Club") (NCCC) are a [first class](/wiki/First-class_cricket "First-class cricket") county [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket") club who play at [Trent Bridge](/wiki/Trent_Bridge "Trent Bridge") in [West Bridgford](/wiki/West_Bridgford "West Bridgford"). They won the [County Championship](/wiki/County_Championship "County Championship") in 2010.

The most successful [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") team within Nottinghamshire is [Nottingham Forest](/wiki/Nottingham_Forest_F.C. "Nottingham Forest F.C."), a [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") club that won the [1978](/wiki/1977%E2%80%9378_Football_League_First_Division "1977–78 Football League First Division") [English championship](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League"), and followed it up with winning the [1979](/wiki/1979_European_Cup_Final "1979 European Cup Final") and [1980](/wiki/1980_European_Cup_Final "1980 European Cup Final") [European Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") titles although success has not been seen by most of their fans in living memory. [Mansfield Town](/wiki/Mansfield_Town_F.C. "Mansfield Town F.C.") of [League One](/wiki/EFL_League_One "EFL League One") and [Notts County](/wiki/Notts_County_F.C. "Notts County F.C.") of [League Two](/wiki/EFL_League_Two "EFL League Two") are the other professional teams in the county. Notts County are the world's oldest professional football club, being formed in 1862 and are world renowned for giving [Juventus](/wiki/Juventus "Juventus") their black and white striped kit and opening their new stadium in 2011.

Other notable sporting teams are the [Nottingham Rugby Football Club](/wiki/Nottingham_R.F.C. "Nottingham R.F.C."), and the [Nottingham Panthers Ice Hockey Club](/wiki/Nottingham_Panthers "Nottingham Panthers").

### Flag

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nottinghamshire&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Flag")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/County_Flag_of_Nottinghamshire.svg/250px-County_Flag_of_Nottinghamshire.svg.png)](/wiki/File:County_Flag_of_Nottinghamshire.svg)

The unofficial flag of Nottinghamshire

Main article: [Flag of Nottinghamshire](/wiki/Flag_of_Nottinghamshire "Flag of Nottinghamshire")

An unofficial flag for Nottinghamshire was created through a design competition organised by [BBC Radio Nottingham](/wiki/BBC_Radio_Nottingham "BBC Radio Nottingham"), and registered with the [vexillological](/wiki/Vexillology "Vexillology") charity the [Flag Institute](/wiki/Flag_Institute "Flag Institute") in 2011. It consists of a green field, on which is a red cross [fimbriated](/wiki/Fimbriation "Fimbriation") (bordered) with white, on which a white shield containing the green figure of an archer is superimposed.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

### Settlements and features

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nottinghamshire&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Settlements and features")]

The traditional county town, and the largest settlement in the historic and ceremonial county boundaries, is the [City of Nottingham](/wiki/Nottingham "Nottingham"). The city is now administratively independent, but towns including [Arnold](/wiki/Arnold,_Nottinghamshire "Arnold, Nottinghamshire"), [Carlton](/wiki/Carlton,_Nottinghamshire "Carlton, Nottinghamshire"), [West Bridgford](/wiki/West_Bridgford "West Bridgford"), [Beeston](/wiki/Beeston,_Nottinghamshire "Beeston, Nottinghamshire"), and [Stapleford](/wiki/Stapleford,_Nottinghamshire "Stapleford, Nottinghamshire") are still within the administrative county, and West Bridgford is where the county council are based.

There are several [market towns](/wiki/Market_town "Market town") in the county. [Newark-on-Trent](/wiki/Newark-on-Trent "Newark-on-Trent") is a bridging point of the [Fosse Way](/wiki/Fosse_Way "Fosse Way") and [River Trent](/wiki/River_Trent "River Trent"), but is actually an Anglo-Saxon market town with a now ruined [castle](/wiki/Newark_Castle,_Nottinghamshire "Newark Castle, Nottinghamshire"). [Mansfield](/wiki/Mansfield "Mansfield"), the second-largest settlement in the county after Nottingham, sits on the site of a [Roman](/wiki/Roman_Britain "Roman Britain") settlement, but grew after the [Norman Conquest](/wiki/Norman_Conquest "Norman Conquest"). [Worksop](/wiki/Worksop "Worksop"), in the north of the county, is also an Anglo-Saxon market town which grew rapidly in the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), with the arrival of [canals](/wiki/Canal "Canal") and railways and the discovery of coal. Other market towns include Arnold, [Bingham](/wiki/Bingham,_Nottinghamshire "Bingham, Nottinghamshire"), [Hucknall](/wiki/Hucknall "Hucknall"), [Kirkby-in-Ashfield](/wiki/Kirkby-in-Ashfield "Kirkby-in-Ashfield"), [Tuxford](/wiki/Tuxford "Tuxford"), [Retford](/wiki/Retford "Retford") and [Sutton-in-Ashfield](/wiki/Sutton-in-Ashfield "Sutton-in-Ashfield").

The main railway in the county is the [Midland Main Line](/wiki/Midland_Main_Line "Midland Main Line"), which links London to [Sheffield](/wiki/Sheffield "Sheffield") via Nottingham. The [Robin Hood Line](/wiki/Robin_Hood_Line "Robin Hood Line") between Nottingham and Worksop serves several villages in the county. The [East Coast Main Line](/wiki/East_Coast_Main_Line "East Coast Main Line") from London to [Doncaster](/wiki/Doncaster "Doncaster"), [Leeds](/wiki/Leeds "Leeds"), [York](/wiki/York "York"), [Newcastle upon Tyne](/wiki/Newcastle_upon_Tyne "Newcastle upon Tyne"), and [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") serves the eastern Nottinghamshire towns of Newark and Retford.

The [M1 motorway](/wiki/M1_motorway "M1 motorway") runs through the county, connecting Nottingham to London, Leeds, and Leicester by road. The [A1 road](/wiki/A1_road_(Great_Britain) "A1 road (Great Britain)") follows for the most part the path of the Great North Road, although in places it diverges from the historic route where towns have been bypassed. Retford was by-passed in 1961, and Newark-on-Trent was by-passed in 1964, and the A1 now runs between Retford and Worksop past the village of [Ranby](/wiki/Ranby,_Nottinghamshire "Ranby, Nottinghamshire"). Many historic [coaching inns](/wiki/Coaching_inn "Coaching inn") can still be seen along the traditional route.

[East Midlands Airport](/wiki/East_Midlands_Airport "East Midlands Airport") is just outside the county in [Leicestershire](/wiki/Leicestershire "Leicestershire"), while [Doncaster Sheffield Airport](/wiki/Doncaster_Sheffield_Airport "Doncaster Sheffield Airport") lies within the historic boundaries of Nottinghamshire. These airports serve the county and several of its neighbours. Together, the airports have services to most major European destinations, and East Midlands Airport now also has services to [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") and the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean"). As well as local bus services throughout the county, Nottingham and its suburbs have a tram system, [Nottingham Express Transit](/wiki/Nottingham_Express_Transit "Nottingham Express Transit").

Nottingham and its surrounding areas form part of the [Nottingham Urban Area](/wiki/Nottingham_Urban_Area "Nottingham Urban Area") while Bassetlaw is a non-constituent part of the Sheffield City Region.
